name: IRT-Aligned Synthetic Responses Support Item Difficulty Prediction With Real-Response Anchors
statement: SMART provides a concrete example of synthetic open-ended responses playing a validity-relevant role in an automated scoring-adjacent workflow: generated responses are useful for item difficulty prediction when trained, aligned, scored, and evaluated against human-scored real-response IRT evidence.
population_or_context: Short-answer reading-comprehension and Java coding items.
quantitative_note: The paper evaluates 49 Smarter Balanced short-answer items and 50 CodeWorkout Java coding items. Table 1 reports SMART PCC/SCC/RMSE of 0.6490/0.4861/0.6699 with Llama-3.2-1B and 0.6737/0.5661/0.6197 with Llama-3.2-3B on Smarter Balanced; on CodeWorkout it reports 0.3082/0.3188/0.6225 with 1B and 0.3929/0.4230/0.5890 with 3B.

source_locator.section: Abstract; Methodology; Experiments; Table 1; Qualitative Evaluation; Limitations
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Extraction uses the three-stage pipeline, human-scored real-response input, DPO/IRT alignment method, and limitations discussion.

caveats: The finding supports item-development and difficulty-estimation evidence, not synthetic-only final scorer validation.
The pipeline relies on human-scored real responses and an LLM-based scoring model whose own operational validity is not established by the synthetic response set alone.
The authors report realism limitations in generated textual distributions.
evaluation_method: Comparison of predicted item difficulties with real-response IRT estimates, plus ability alignment, feature-distribution checks, qualitative response comparisons, and failure-pattern analysis.
limitations: Small item counts and private dataset constraints limit generalization.
Synthetic response realism and scoring-model validity remain separate evidentiary questions.
